title: GDPR consent-bypass and retention drift in analytics pipeline

details.what: The analytics pipeline in src/analytics.js sends raw PII (email, IP address, session ID) to an external vendor endpoint without a GDPR consent gate, and the advertised 30-day data retention is never enforced because the purge function is dead code. The published privacy policy in docs/privacy.md contradicts the implementation on consent basis, identifier hashing, and retention enforcement.
details.why: This exposes the application to GDPR Article 6 violations (no lawful basis for processing), Article 5(1)(e) violations (storage limitation principle breached), and inability to fulfill Data Subject Access Requests (Articles 15–22). The drift between published policy and actual code behavior constitutes a regulatory and security risk that could result in supervisory authority action.

title: Enforce GDPR consent check and data retention controls in analytics event pipeline

details.what: Gate analytics event dispatch on explicit user consent, hash PII before transmission, and schedule the existing purgeStaleLogs function to enforce the 30-day retention window advertised in the privacy policy.
details.why: The logEvent function unconditionally POSTs raw email and IP to an external vendor without checking user.gdpr_consent, violating the explicit opt-in consent model described in docs/privacy.md. The purgeStaleLogs function exists but is never called, so the 30-day retention promise is unenforced.
details.who: Backend engineers maintaining the analytics module and the privacy/compliance stakeholders who authored docs/privacy.md.

title: Add consent gate to logEvent, hash PII before transmission, and schedule purgeStaleLogs to enforce 30-day retention

details.what: In src/analytics.js, the logEvent function (lines 25–38) unconditionally POSTs raw PII — user.email and request.ip — to the external vendor at ANALYTICS_URL without checking user.gdpr_consent. The purgeStaleLogs function (lines 44–48) is defined but never scheduled, so the 30-day data retention policy advertised in docs/privacy.md is never enforced. The privacy policy also claims identifiers are hashed, but logEvent sends them in cleartext.
details.why: This is a GDPR Article 6(1)(a) violation: the privacy policy states processing is based on explicit opt-in consent, but the code never checks consent status before transmitting PII. Additionally, GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) is breached because the purge mechanism is dead code — vendor-side data persists indefinitely (90 days per the code comment on line 28 vs. the 30-day policy). A Data Subject Access Request under Articles 15–22 cannot be authoritatively answered because there is no audit trail of what was sent. The gap between docs/privacy.md and src/analytics.js means the published privacy policy is materially false.
details.who: AGENT implements the code fix; privacy/compliance stakeholder reviews the updated docs/privacy.md alignment.
details.when: Before next production deployment — this is a live data-protection violation with regulatory exposure.
details.where: src/analytics.js:25-38 (logEvent — consent bypass and raw PII), src/analytics.js:44-48 (purgeStaleLogs — dead retention code), docs/privacy.md:12-17 (30-day retention claim), docs/privacy.md:22-27 (explicit consent claim).
details.how: 1. Add an early-return guard in logEvent: if (!user.gdpr_consent) return; 2. Hash user.email with SHA-256 before including in payload to match the policy's 'hashed user ID' claim. 3. Replace raw request.ip with a truncated/hashed form or remove it from the payload. 4. Wire purgeStaleLogs to a cron schedule (e.g., daily) so the 30-day retention window is actively enforced. 5. Update docs/privacy.md if the implementation diverges from the stated policy after fixes.
details.constraints: Must not break existing analytics consumers that depend on the event_type and timestamp fields. The consent check must handle the case where user.gdpr_consent is undefined (pre-existing sessions). The hash algorithm must match what docs/privacy.md describes. The purge schedule must run in the existing deployment environment (no new infrastructure dependency).

cost.blast_radius: analytics pipeline — all tracked events will be gated; vendor-side purge affects historical data

5m.man: 1 backend engineer to implement consent gate + hashing + cron wiring; 1 privacy stakeholder to review policy alignment
5m.machine: existing cron/scheduler infrastructure for purgeStaleLogs; no new services required
5m.material: Node.js crypto module for SHA-256 hashing (built-in, no new dependency)
5m.method: direct edit of src/analytics.js to add consent guard and hash transform; cron job configuration for purge; integration test asserting no POST when gdpr_consent is false/undefined
5m.measurement: unit test: logEvent with gdpr_consent=false emits zero fetch calls; unit test: logEvent with gdpr_consent=true emits hashed email, no raw IP; integration test: purgeStaleLogs runs on schedule and issues DELETE to vendor; manual review: docs/privacy.md statements match implementation

evidence.snippet: Personal identifiers (IP address, direct identifiers) are retained for a **maximum of 30 days**, after which they are either deleted or replaced with a salted hash that cannot be reversed to the source identifier.

We rely on **explicit opt-in consent** (GDPR Article 6(1)(a)) for analytics and marketing processing. You may withdraw consent at any time via your account settings; withdrawal takes effect immediately and suppresses all subsequent analytics events from the affected session.
